coredata.dc:title: Statistical analysis for robust battery state estimation: Demonstrating ANOVA-driven feature selection with electrochemical impedance spectroscopy

coredata.dc:description: Accurate state estimation is essential for the safe and efficient operation of lithium-ion batteries, with temperature being a key variable affecting performance and degradation. electrochemical impedance spectroscopy-based temperature estimation has been widely explored due to its advantages over surface-mounted sensors. However, systematic feature selection methods capable of quantifying the effect of all variance sources on impedance, including their interactions, have not yet been reported. Here, we propose a feature selection approach based on analysis of variance (ANOVA). The method calculates the effect size (ES) of all variance sources and their interactions for each impedance feature. Since ES quantifies relative contributions, it enables objective comparison and ranking of features. Although demonstrated here for temperature estimation, the method can be applied to any state variable. Validation with synthetic data from a Doyle-Fuller-Newman-based model confirmed the ability to identify features primarily driven by temperature. The method was then applied to experimental data across varying temperatures and state of charge (SoC). Finally, three features of different ranks were tested under dynamic conditions, where the highest-ranked feature yielded the lowest root-mean-square error of 1.25°C without SoC information. This systematic approach advances impedance-based diagnostics by improving state estimation accuracy and providing a standardized feature selection framework.

Overview of the most relevant approaches for temperature estimation based on EIS found in the literature. The table includes the features used, considered temperature range, cell chemistry and format, capacity, and the principal author of each study. It provides a comprehensive overview of the heterogeneity in selected features for temperature estimation, as well as the number of studies published on the topic over the last two decades.

head.ce:title: Statistical analysis for robust battery state estimation: Demonstrating ANOVA-driven feature selection with electrochemical impedance spectroscopy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate state estimation is essential for the safe and efficient operation of lithium-ion batteries, with temperature being a key variable affecting performance and degradation. electrochemical impedance spectroscopy-based temperature estimation has been widely explored due to its advantages over surface-mounted sensors. However, systematic feature selection methods capable of quantifying the effect of all variance sources on impedance, including their interactions, have not yet been reported. Here, we propose a feature selection approach based on analysis of variance (ANOVA). The method calculates the effect size (ES) of all variance sources and their interactions for each impedance feature. Since ES quantifies relative contributions, it enables objective comparison and ranking of features. Although demonstrated here for temperature estimation, the method can be applied to any state variable. Validation with synthetic data from a Doyle-Fuller-Newman-based model confirmed the ability to identify features primarily driven by temperature. The method was then applied to experimental data across varying temperatures and state of charge (SoC). Finally, three features of different ranks were tested under dynamic conditions, where the highest-ranked feature yielded the lowest root-mean-square error of 1.25°C without SoC information. This systematic approach advances impedance-based diagnostics by improving state estimation accuracy and providing a standardized feature selection framework.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) has garnered sustained interest within the lithium-ion battery (LIB) research community [1], primarily due to its capacity to elucidate a cell’s kinetics, providing insight into its thermal and electrochemical state.

Building on this advantage of EIS, a substantial number of studies have examined a variety of methods for diagnosing state of health (SoH) [2–4], state of charge (SoC) [5,6], and internal temperature [7–17], ranging from simple manual mapping of certain attributes in impedance vectors to sensitivity-based methods and sophisticated and purely data-driven approaches.

Specifically, impedance-based temperature estimation has been widely investigated, with numerous approaches published over the past two decades [7–11,13–17]. This growing interest is primarily driven by the key advantages of impedance-based temperature estimation over traditional surface mounted temperature sensors, including the absence of thermal delay and the ability to estimate the internal mean temperature of the cell [11,17]. Additionally, impedance-based temperature estimation enables monitoring the temperature of each cell connected in series by using the individual voltage signals already measured by the battery management system (BMS). This is especially relevant in large systems, where the number of cells, often hundreds or even thousands, makes the installation of temperature sensors in every cell impractical [18,19].

Since cell temperature monitoring must be performed in real time, most studies focus on single- or few-frequency approaches for temperature estimation to reduce the measurement and computation time required by methods based on equivalent circuit model (ECM) fitting [20]. This represents a perfect example of a simple feature selection problem, where only one feature, i.e., an impedance component at a specific frequency [7,8,11,13], or the intercept frequency
f
0
[17], is selected for temperature estimation. Since impedance is a complex quantity, four impedance components can be defined: the real part of the impedance
ℜ
( Z )
[11–13], the imaginary part
ℑ
( Z )
[13–15], the magnitude
| Z |
, and the phase shift
ϕ
( Z )
[7–10].

The problem definition is quite straightforward: identify features that are maximally sensitive to temperature while minimally affected by other independent variables (IVs), such as SoC and SoH. However, the literature presents a wide variety of selected features for temperature estimation. Table 1 provides an overview of the most relevant EIS-based approaches for temperature estimation, including the selected features, temperature ranges, chemistries, formats, capacities, and lead authors. This overview underscores both the heterogeneity of feature choices and the volume of related publications.

One could argue that this diversity in feature selection stems from differences in cell chemistries, formats, or capacities. However, a closer review reveals a lack of systematic, reproducible strategies for selecting features in impedance-based temperature estimation.

In most published methods, the feature selection process relies on measuring the impedance spectra under different temperature, SoC, and, in some cases, SoH conditions. Afterward, impedance spectra are typically inspected visually to identify impedance components at certain frequencies or other features like
f
0
that exhibit high sensitivity to temperature and low sensitivity to other IVs [10–13,15,17].

This feature selection approach presents multiple limitations. First, interactions between different IVs are difficult to account for, as the impedance spectra must be visually inspected across several combinations of the IVs’ values. The difficulty of this analysis increases multiplicatively with the number of IVs, becoming impractical for more than two IVs and four impedance components. Second, the visual analysis is highly subjective and lacks quantifiable effect attribution. Authors often use terms like “almost entirely independent of SoC”, indicating a lack of rigorous quantification of the IV’s effect on the features. Finally, the final feature selection often relies on user judgment, introducing bias and reducing reproducibility.

Zhu et al. [8] proposed a more systematic method for feature selection based on identifying the boundaries of frequency ranges in which impedance is dominated by a certain factor, such as temperature, while being relatively insensitive to others, such as SoC. Based on these boundaries, multiple “frequency selection matrices” were constructed. By combining these matrices with impedance matrices corresponding to different impedance components, temperature estimation was performed. However, this method still relies on visual interpretation of the impedance spectra and therefore suffers from the aforementioned limitations.

Li et al. [15] highlighted that the effects of SoC and particularly SoH are often not properly quantified for feature selection in temperature estimation. They introduced a multi-frequency,
ℑ
( Z )
-based temperature estimation method using a Gaussian process regression (GPR) model. An ECM was fitted to impedance data collected across varying temperature, SoC, and SoH conditions. After analyzing the individual effect of each IV on ECM parameters through sensitivity analysis, they computed Pearson’s correlation coefficients between temperature, SoC, and SoH and each impedance component at different frequencies. Based on these correlations, they selected
ℑ
( Z )
at 26.83, 273.27, and 377.45Hz as the most suitable features for temperature estimation. However, their sensitivity analysis is based on a one-variable-at-a-time approach and does not capture interactions between temperature, SoC, and SoH. Similarly, the Pearson’s correlation coefficients calculated for each frequency only reflect linear relationships and do not account for joint effects between IVs.

Beelen et al. [16] identified similar limitations in only comparing the impedance sensitivity to temperature (
∂ Z
∂ T
) and SoC (
∂ Z
∂ SoC
) for feature selection in temperature estimation. They proposed an approach based on Monte-Carlo simulation to identify the most suitable combination of impedance components and frequencies. Their approach evaluates the bias and standard deviation of the estimated temperature for a range of frequencies, temperatures, and SoCs, including measurement noise. This method showed improved accuracy compared to previous state-of-the-art approaches. However, this methodology is inherently dependent on the specific estimator and the battery model used in the simulation. In other words, it does not directly analyze the intrinsic sensitivity of individual features to temperature, SoC and possible further IVs, such as SoH, but rather the performance of the model-estimator combination. Moreover, interpreting the simulation results requires manual inspection and decision-making, often involving a trade-off between sensitivity, bias, and variance. This interpretive burden grows significantly with the number of IVs, as the number of possible combinations increases multiplicatively.

To the best of our knowledge, no study has included the effect of cell-to-cell variation in the feature selection. This represents a significant gap, as intrinsic differences between cells of the same type do not necessarily impact all features uniformly [27]. Similarly, the measuring equipment may affect the features in an uneven manner, leading to an influence on the feature selection results.

Motivated by these limitations in the current research landscape, we propose a feature selection methodology based on the well-established statistical tool analysis of variance (ANOVA). While the methodology is applied here to temperature estimation, it can be generalized to any state estimation task using EIS data. Therefore, the objective of this proposal is twofold: (1) to demonstrate the use of ANOVA for interpretable and quantitative feature selection in the context of impedance-based temperature estimation, and (2) to contribute to LIB research by offering a novel perspective on the subject of feature extraction for EIS-based state estimation.

To the best of our knowledge, ANOVA has not been applied to feature selection in the context of EIS-based state estimation. Xiao et al. [28] used ANOVA to evaluate the significance of temperature and SoC on different ECM parameters. Nonetheless, the authors restricted their analysis to a comparison of the resulting p -values for temperature and SoC to support their qualitative observations about the measured impedance spectra under varying temperature and SoC conditions. No feature selection for state estimation was performed based on the ANOVA results, and no quantitative analysis of the IV’s effect on the features was conducted.

Our proposed method applies ANOVA to a set of features measured across varying temperature and SoC conditions. ANOVA results are then used to compute the effect size (ES) of the temperature and SoC on every feature. Since the effect size (ES) quantifies the relative contribution of each IV to the total variance, the feature selection becomes a straightforward task, where the feature with the highest ES for temperature shows the lowest relative amount of variance explained by the SoC and cell-to-cell variation. Finally, we test this methodology using a separate dataset collected under dynamic temperature and SoC conditions.

In this context, this paper is structured as follows: (i) We introduce the fundamentals of ANOVA and its mathematical formulations. (ii) We present the application of ANOVA and demonstrate its capability to quantify the ES of the different variance sources in a physicochemical impedance model based on the Doyle–Fuller Newman model. (iii) We introduce the ANOVA results corresponding to an EIS dataset measured under isothermal conditions and varying SoC for a real LIB cell and provide a feature selection for temperature estimation. (iv) We inspect the performance of the selected feature using a separate dataset measured under dynamic temperature and SoC conditions. Finally, we conclude the paper with a summary of the findings and an outlook on future work.

ANOVA tests whether the effect associated with a change in the value of an independent variable (IV) is statistically significant [29, p. 71]. An IV is a factor that may or may not affect the value of a dependent variable (DV) [29, p. 2]. In an experimental design, researchers define the levels of the IV while measuring the scores of the DV. Within the context of battery research, an IV can be the internal cell temperature, set through conditioning with a temperature chamber, or the specific SoC. An example of a DV is the impedance
Z
( f )
at a given frequency f . The levels of an IV refer to the specific values of the IV set during the experiment, for example, the temperatures set during impedance measurements.

In a fully crossed or full factorial design of experiments (DoE), the subjects (in the context of this work, the lithium-ion cells) are measured under all possible combinations of all levels of the IVs. Each of these combinations is referred to as a treatment [29, p. 480] [30, p. 246].

To enhance the readability of this section and to align the terminology of ANOVA with the context of battery research, we illustrate the discussion with specific examples. Throughout this section, the internal cell temperature and the SoC are used as examples of IVs, while the measured impedance serves as an example of a DV. It is important to note that the mathematical formulations and statistical principles presented are general and can be applied to any choice of independent and dependent variables. Moreover, Table 2 provides a summary of the key ANOVA terms and illustrative examples from the field of battery research.

ANOVA splits the total deviation between each impedance measurement and the grand mean (GM) over all impedance measurements into two components: First, the deviation of each treatment’s mean from the GM. Second, the deviation between each impedance measurement and the mean of all impedance measurements belonging to the same treatment. This can be expressed in terms of the sum of squares (SSQ) as
(1)

Considering only one IV (e.g., internal cell temperature), the first term on the right hand side in Eq. (1) can be interpreted as the effect of the temperature, and the second term as the deviation within each temperature level, usually denoted in the literature as “error” [29, p. 75].

However, within the context of Eq. (1), these error terms do not solely represent measurement inaccuracies. Instead, these error terms indicate deviations that the variation of the temperature cannot explain [31]. In this study, an example of such error is the variation observed among the lithium-ion cells, potentially due to manufacturing heterogeneity.

The total number of impedance measurements can be expressed as the product of the number of temperature levels a and the number of impedance measurements per temperature level n , assuming that the same number of lithium-ion cells were measured under each temperature level. Additionally,

Furthermore, the variance, also referred to as mean square (MS), is defined as the SSQ divided by the corresponding DF. Accordingly, MS values are computed as follows [29, p. 76]:

ANOVA defines the F value for temperature as the ratio of the variance associated with the temperature to the variance driven by all other sources of variation different from temperature, as expressed in Eq. (7) [29, pp. 75–77].
(7)

The F -ratio is used to test the null hypothesis (
H
0
) [29, p. 77]. The null hypothesis states that the means of all treatments are equal and, consequently, that temperature has no effect on impedance [30, p. 34]. Once the F -ratio for temperature has been computed, it is compared against a critical F -ratio, typically obtained from F -distribution tables. These tables are indexed by degrees of freedom, with the row corresponding to
DF
T
and the column to
DF
error
. If the F -ratio computed for temperature is equal to or greater than the critical F -ratio,
H
0
is rejected, indicating that temperature has a statistically significant effect on impedance. Thus, a larger F -ratio increases the probability of rejecting a false
H
0
. [29, p. 77]

Intuitively, if temperature has no impact on impedance, the variance due to temperature will be approximately equal to the variance driven by all other sources of variation, resulting in an F -ratio close to 1. Both an increase in the effect of temperature and a decrease in error contribute to a higher F -ratio.

Rather than relying on the F -ratio for
H
0
testing, ANOVA usually focuses on the p -value, which indicates the probability of observing the measured variance in impedance if
H
0
were actually true [30,32]. A widely accepted convention is to reject
H
0
when the probability is below 5% (
p < 0 . 05
), leading to the conclusion that the IV has a statistically significant impact [30–35]. In this work, the same threshold of 0.05 is adopted to establish statistical significance.

Additionally, when the impedance of each lithium-ion cell has been measured under all possible combinations of the levels of the IVs, the design receives the name of within-subjects design or repeated-measures design [29,31]. This design shows several advantages with respect to between-groups designs, in which different groups of subjects receive different treatments. For example, the repeated-measures design allows to account for differences between the cells and is more efficient since less lithium-ion cells and resources are necessary [29, pp. 7–8].

Since in repeated-measures designs all lithium-ion cells are measured under all possible combinations of the levels of the IVs, the differences between the cells can be analyzed as a potential source of variability in impedance [29, p. 249]. Therefore,
SSQ
error
in Eq. (2) can be divided into the variability due to cell-to-cell differences
SSQ
S
and the remaining variability interpreted as the error term [29, p. 250].

Therefore, if we now consider two IVs, temperature and SoC,

Similarly to Eqs. (5) and (6), the MS values for temperature, SoC, and their interaction can be calculated. The corresponding F -ratios are then determined as follows:

It is noteworthy that ANOVA is also capable of evaluating
H
0
for the interaction between temperature and SoC. In other words, ANOVA can evaluate not only the statistical significance of varying an IV, such as temperature or SoC, but also the interaction between them when both IVs change simultaneously. An interaction occurs, for example, when temperature does not have a consistent effect on impedance across all levels of SoC [30, p. 4]. The evaluation of this interaction’s significance is usually neglected in the literature, specially when the effect of more than two IVs is analyzed [8,10,14,15,25].

Because each lithium-ion cell is measured under all temperature levels, the errors in a repeated-measures ANOVA are inherently correlated, so the usual assumption of independent errors does not hold. Instead, the analysis assumes sphericity, meaning that the variances of the differences in impedance between all possible pairs of temperature levels are equal. When sphericity does not hold, the probability of a Type I error increases, i.e., incorrectly rejecting a true
H
0
. [29, pp. 284, 285]. To address this, the Greenhouse-Geisser (GG) correction is applied in this work. This correction computes an adjustment factor, ϵ , that reflects the degree to which sphericity is not satisfied. The factor ϵ reduces the DF used for calculating both the numerator and denominator in Eqs. (10)–(12), yielding more conservative p -values (
p
GG
) for testing
H
0
. [29, p. 287] Therefore, in this work an IV is considered to have a statistically significant effect on impedance when
p
GG
< 0 . 05
.

However, evaluating the
p
GG
-value against a predefined significance threshold, and thus rejecting or failing to reject
H
0
, is not sufficient to address the significance of varying temperature on impedance. This limitation is a common critique of conventional hypothesis testing [32].

As Kirk [32] and others have emphasized, what
H
0
significance testing provides is the probability of obtaining the measured impedance, or more extreme variance values, assuming
H
0
is true [32], [30, p. 37]. However, in scientific inference, what we often seek is the inverse: the probability that
H
0
is true given the measured dataset [32].

Additionally, the problem addressed in this work extends beyond assessing whether a IV such as temperature has a statistically significant effect on a certain impedance feature. Our approach aims to compare the relative significance of different impedance features (see Section 4), and based on this comparison, identify the most suitable feature for estimating the underlying IV, interpreted as the causal source of variance in the observed impedance feature scores.

Therefore, in this work it is also necessary to evaluate the ES of temperature, SoC, and their interaction.
ES
T
can be defined as the ratio of the variance explained by temperature to the total variance observed in impedance. In other words, it quantifies the relative contribution of temperature to the total variance in impedance measurements [31]. While statistical significance addresses a dichotomous accept–reject decision about the significance of temperature,
ES
T
provides a measure of the relative strength of the temperature effect [31,36].

Several measures of ES have been proposed in the literature. In this work, we focus on omega squared (
ω
ˆ
2
), as it is less biased than more commonly reported measures such as eta squared (
η
2
), and therefore less likely to overestimate the ES of an IV or interaction. This property is particularly relevant when working with small sample sizes, which are often unavoidable in battery research [31].

Since ANOVA compares group means across discrete temperature levels, the non-linear relationship between temperature and impedance may lead to misleading results, as such non-linearities can obscure the true effect of temperature on impedance. To address this issue, the number of temperature levels in the DoE should be guided by prior knowledge of the expected relationship between temperature and impedance.

For example, we know that impedance increases at low temperatures, decreases at moderate and high temperatures, and rises again at extremely high temperatures [37,38]. Consequently, the mean impedance at low and extremely high temperatures could be similar. A two-level design with only low and extremely high temperature levels would not capture this parabolic behavior, because the intermediate decrease in impedance at moderate temperatures would not be measured. ANOVA would, in this case, report no temperature effect even though impedance clearly depends on it. Including an intermediate temperature level would reveal the effect, as the impedance of the middle group would differ from the extremes.

In general, increasing the number of levels of an IV improves the ability of ANOVA to capture non-linear relationships between that IV and impedance. However, this increased resolution comes at the cost of higher experimental complexity and resource requirements. Therefore, when designing the DoE, a balance must be found between adequately resolving potential non-linearities and maintaining practical feasibility.

A practical recommendation is to support this decision through a preliminary assessment of the relationship between the IV and impedance. For commonly investigated IVs such as temperature, SoC, or SoH, extensive prior studies in the literature already provide valuable insight into their typical relationship with impedance and can therefore serve as a useful starting point for selecting appropriate levels in the DoE.

If such prior knowledge is unavailable or insufficient, preliminary exploratory measurements can be performed to characterize the qualitative shape of the relationship between the IV and impedance. Based on these observations, non-linear behaviors can be explicitly taken into account when defining the DoE.

Finally, once the non-linear relationship between an IV and impedance has been sufficiently resolved in the DoE, further increasing the number of levels of that IV is not expected to significantly alter the ANOVA results. This observation can serve as a simple sensitivity check, indicating that the chosen level resolution is adequate for robust and consistent ANOVA results.

For application in lithium-ion batteries, the presented formulation of ANOVA must accurately distinguish between variances caused by different treatments, which can be perceived as different electrochemical or thermal states of the cell in the presented work. For an initial, theoretical analysis, we investigate ANOVA with a physicochemical impedance model based on the well-known Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) model in the frequency domain, originally presented by Doyle et al. [39] and further elaborated by Natterer et al. [40]. For the reader’s convenience, we will shortly discuss the fundamental idea of this model and its physical implications. For more details, we recommend the publications by Natterer et al. [40] and Doyle et al. [39].

Starting from the DFN in the time domain, further referenced with DFN(t) [41,42], the extension of every considered state variable, describing, e.g., concentration and potential in the considered cell domains, by an imaginary term enables the model to replicate the frequency domain behavior under the inherent assumption of linearity, which is reflected in the excitation’s unit amplitude but also the Kramers–Kronig conditions [43–45]. Eq. (17) summarizes this approach for an arbitrary state variable X .
(17)
X
real
=
X
̄
+ Re
[
X
̃
exp ι ω t ]
X
imag
=
Im
[
X
̃
exp i ω t ]

In addition, computationally intensive, iterative approximation of the particles’ concentration patterns can be omitted by using an analytically solvable representation of the solid phase’s diffusion term in the frequency domain. This procedure facilitates the model-solving process, which is reduced to solving a discretization-dependent linear equation system [39,46,47] [preprint JN]. This significantly reduces the computation time and we could previously show, that this approach is able to replicate impedance values in a fraction of the required time of a DFN(t) with time-resolved excitation and Fast Fourier transformation without loss of accuracy [47] [preprint JN].

In terms of methodological validation of ANOVA, the DFN(f) enables the precise construction of a well-defined, synthetic, yet physically meaningful impedance dataset through the a priori integration of known temperature dependencies, inherent SoC conditions, and cell-to-cell variance. Since the model studied and proposed by Natterer et al. [40] already accounts for SoC variations, temperature dependencies were incorporated in the form of a simple Arrhenius relation, which modifies the solid-phase diffusion constant as a function of the chosen temperature. In addition to accounting for the temperature dependence of solid-phase diffusion, we neglect the temperature dependence of other factors, such as charge-transfer impedance and electrolyte properties, in order to clearly isolate the different variance sources in the ANOVA results. It is important to emphasize that, while this modeling approach is not physically comprehensive, it provides the controlled environment necessary for testing and validating the ANOVA methodology, as the boundary frequency range of the temperature dependence is known a priori.

Moreover, each simulated impedance vector was postprocessed after simulation with uncorrelated Gaussian noise of
σ = 2 %
to mimic cell-to-cell variation. Again, this is not physically comprehensive, since real cell-to-cell variances are likely caused by parameter and structural variations within the cell, such as differences in coating thickness or porosity. Nevertheless, this simplified approach facilitates the validation of ANOVA by approximating physical variability without introducing unnecessary computational complexity.
By using this adapted DFN(f) model, a large number of synthetic impedance spectra with known temperature dependence and defined cell-to-cell variance could be generated computationally.

In this study, a total of seven lithium-ion cells with a nominal capacity of 4.500mAh, manufactured by Molicel (E-One Moli Energy Corp., Taiwan) in the 21700 format, were utilized. These cells incorporated a nickel cobalt aluminum (NCA) cathode and a graphite anode, blended with silicon oxide. For the experimental measurements, the cells were equipped with additional tabs, welded to the terminals for constant electrical contact over the course of the study.

To collect isothermal data, four of the cells described in Section 3.1 were subjected to an identical reference performance test (RPT) at 5°C, 25°C, and 45°C. Cells were mounted horizontally in a climate chamber, which maintained the desired ambient temperature within ± 1°C.

Since the RPT includes charging steps, the lower bound temperature was selected in consideration of the minimum charging temperature of 0°C specified in the cell manufacturer’s datasheet. Similarly, the upper bound temperature was chosen to be well below the maximum charging temperature of 60°C indicated in the datasheet. In addition, 45°C is comparable to the upper temperature limits commonly investigated in the literature on temperature estimation based on EIS, as summarized in Table 1, with the exception of studies focusing on electrical or thermal abuse [13,22]. Furthermore, 45°C covers the typical maximum operating temperature defined for numerous battery applications [48,49]. The selection of three temperature levels was motivated by the need to capture non-linearities in the relationship between temperature and impedance, as discussed in Section 2.1, while keeping the experimental effort manageable.

The RPT consisted of an initial C/5 charge and discharge regime between 2.5V and 4.2V. When the cell potential reached the upper voltage limit during constant current (CC) charging, the regime changed to constant voltage (CV) until the current dropped below C/30. Only CC was used for discharging to 2.5V.

Additionally, a pseudo-open-circuit voltage (OCV) measurement was performed with C/30 in both charge and discharge directions. The obtained capacity was used to set the SoC steps between 0.9 and 0.1 with a resolution of 0.1 for SoC-resolved EIS measurements. Similar to temperature, the nine equally distributed SoC levels were selected to capture the typically non-linear and frequency-dependent relationship between SoC and impedance [5,6], while keeping the experimental time manageable. Additionally, we consider that the selected SoC range covers the recommended boundaries in numerous battery systems used in practical applications [50,51].

Prior to each EIS measurement, the cell was rested for at least 1h to ensure both thermal and electrochemical steady states. EIS was performed galvanostatically using an amplitude of 390mA and a frequency window of 500kHz to 25mHz, logarithmically resolved with 8 points per decade. The impedance of each frequency was measured 9 times and averaged to ensure consistency. EIS was performed using the VMP-300 potentiostat from Biologic Science Instruments SAS.
The resulting dataset represents a full factorial DoE with three temperature levels and nine SoC levels, in which impedance was measured under all possible combinations of temperature and SoC.

To test the proposed methodology for feature selection, three of the cells described in Section 3.1 were subjected to an identical temperature profile under varying SoC conditions. Similarly to the isothermal measurements (Section 3.2), cells were mounted horizontally in a climate chamber for temperature control. EIS was performed using the VMP-3 potentiostat from Biologic Science Instruments SAS. For temperature monitoring, PT100 temperature sensors were attached to the cells’ surface at their mid-length.

Prior to the experiment, an initial capacity estimation was performed at 25°C. This consisted of a C/3 CC discharge phase followed by a C/3 CC charge phase between 2.5V and 4.2V. Subsequently, the cells were discharged at C/3 to an SoC of 0.8, from which point the experiment was initiated. The cell capacity measured during the charge phase was used to set the SoC steps between 0.8 and 0.2 with a resolution of 0.2 during the experiment. Since exact SoC estimation was not required for this experiment, a faster capacity estimation method than that described in Section 3.2 was used.

During the experiment, three temperature steps, 10°C, 25°C, and 40°C, were applied to the cells. The motivation for selecting these temperature levels was to evaluate the performance of the selected feature for temperature estimation at levels different from those in the isothermal measurement dataset. Each temperature step had a duration of approximately 5h, counted from the point when the temperature chamber setting was changed to the next temperature. At the midpoint of each temperature step, the cells were discharged with C/3 to the next SoC step.

Throughout the experiment, EIS was continuously performed on the cells with a 5min pause between measurement repetitions. During SoC variations, no EIS measurements were performed. The selected frequencies correspond to the features selected using ANOVA and are discussed in Section 5.3. EIS was performed galvanostatically using an amplitude of 390mA. The impedance of each frequency was measured 9 times and averaged to ensure consistency.

In this work, an impedance feature is defined as an impedance component,
ℜ
( Z )
,
− ℑ
( Z )
,
| Z |
, or
ϕ
( Z )
, measured at a certain frequency, or alternatively the intercept frequency
f
0
. These features have been predominantly used in the literature for temperature estimation.

Once the input dataset is structured by grouping all combinations of the IVs’ levels with the corresponding DV scores, ANOVA is applied to each feature using MATLAB’s ranova and fitrm modules for repeated-measures designs. For each feature, an ANOVA table similar to Table 3 is obtained. This exemplary table corresponds to
ℜ
( Z )
at 850.275Hz for the dataset obtained from the isothermal measurements.

In the first step, the impedance model described in Section 2.2 was used to test the ANOVA implementation before using real impedance data. This model allows for defining the amount of deviation between the “cells”, where “cells” refer, in the context of the model, to the simulation iterations, and the amount of variance due to temperature, applied to a certain range of the frequency spectrum. This allows for a simple yet robust comparison between the expected ESs of both variance sources and the ANOVA output, supporting and validating the development of our ANOVA framework for impedance data.

Subsequently, ANOVA was applied to every feature using the isothermal dataset (see Section 3.2), with temperature and SoC as the IVs. The obtained
p
GG
values were then used for testing
H
0
(
p
GG
< 0 . 05
). Additionally, based on the output ANOVA tables,
ω
ˆ
T
2
and
ω
ˆ
SoC
2
were calculated and a ranking from highest to lowest ES for temperature and SoC was derived, respectively.

In addition to the MATLAB implementation, ANOVA results for the isothermal dataset were replicated and verified using the Python package statsmodels and its anova_lm function. The GG correction, as described in Section 2.1, was applied manually, since the statsmodels package does not provide a built-in option for this adjustment.

Additionally, a dynamic temperature and SoC profile was applied to three different lithium-ion cells of the same type as those used in the isothermal measurements. If the derivations from the isothermal dataset are valid, an accurate temperature tracking must be feasible with features showing dominant ESs. During these non-isothermal measurements, temperature was estimated using three different features: The highest and a mid-ranked ES feature in the temperature ranking, and the highest ES feature in the SoC ranking, which consequently is one of the lowest-ranked features in the temperature ranking.
Finally, the root-mean-square error (RMSE) between the real and estimated temperature was used to evaluate the use of ANOVA as a feature selection approach for impedance-based temperature estimation.

The theoretical, experimental, and methodological considerations discussed in Sections 2, 3, and 4 now allow us to investigate the performance of ANOVA in a twofold manner: first, we validate the methodology by applying it to a synthetic impedance dataset generated using the impedance model described in Section 2.2; second, we apply ANOVA to experimental impedance data to evaluate its performance as a feature selection approach for impedance-based temperature estimation.

Fig. 1 presents the impedance model results for three different temperatures, with 20 iterations incorporating a Gaussian noise of
σ = 2 %
to represent cell-to-cell variation as described in Section 2.2. Error bars indicate the standard deviation, while points visualize the mean values across the 20 iterations. The cell-to-cell variation remains constant throughout the frequency spectrum. In contrast, temperature-induced variation is primarily present in the middle and low-frequency regions, beginning at approximately 500Hz and 50Hz for
− ℑ
( Z )
and
ℜ
( Z )
, respectively, and increases as frequency decreases.

The differences in temperature dependence between
ℜ
( Z )
and
ℑ
( Z )
arise from the temperature dependencies implemented in the model through an Arrhenius relationship. This relationship specifically affects predominantly the solid-phase diffusion constant as a function of temperature, as detailed in Section 2.2.

The predominance of the different variance sources in the different well-controlled frequency regions represent an ideal test case for the ANOVA implementation, since the ANOVA results can be expected to show a clear distinction between the variance due to temperature and the variance due to cell-to-cell differences in the different frequency regions.

Fig. 2(e–h) presents the ANOVA results for the synthetic impedance dataset. To enable a clear and direct comparison between the ANOVA results and the expected ES of the variance sources, Fig. 2(a–d) illustrates the variance due to temperature and cell-to-cell differences as functions of frequency. The variance due to temperature in Fig. 2(a) and (b) is depicted as the average across the three simulated temperatures, where each impedance value corresponds to the mean over the 20 simulation iterations at that temperature.

On the other hand, the variance due to cell-to-cell differences shown in Fig. 2(c) and (d) is represented as the mean over the 20 iterations at the middle temperature. The shaded area around the mean line again indicates the standard deviation. This visualization provides a comprehensive understanding of how the sources of variance manifest across the frequency spectrum and supports the interpretation of the ANOVA results.

It can be observed that the ANOVA results align with the expected ES of the variance sources. ANOVA accurately distinguishes between the variance due to temperature and the variance due to cell-to-cell differences across different frequency regions. The boundary frequencies at which the temperature variance becomes significant, highlighted in Fig. 1, correspond to those where
p
GG
values are lower than 0.05, which is commonly defined as the critical threshold in ANOVA for considering the variation of an IV to be statistically significant (Fig. 2(e) and (f)).

Furthermore, the variance attributed to temperature, as quantified by the ES, consistently reflects the pattern of temperature-induced impedance variation, increasing from the boundary frequencies (highlighted in Fig. 1) toward lower frequencies, as shown in Fig. 2(g) and (h). These results indicate that the ANOVA implementation is capable of accurately quantifying the ES of the well-defined sources of variance thereby validating its effectiveness and suitability for this application.

These results demonstrate the potential of ANOVA as a feature selection approach for the investigation of impedance data, obtained under various operating conditions. On one hand, the
p
GG
values obtained from ANOVA for both variance sources allow for evaluation of the statistical significance of the temperature variation. On the other hand, the ES values quantify the relative strength of the temperature effect with respect to the total variance in the impedance data. This dual approach enables a comprehensive understanding of the relationship between temperature and impedance, facilitating the identification of relevant features for temperature estimation.

Once the ANOVA implementation was validated and deemed adequate for feature importance and distinguishability analysis using the well-defined, synthetic dataset from the DFN(f), ANOVA was applied to measurement results obtained from the isothermal setup (Section 3.2).

Fig. 3 resume the results of the isothermal measurements for every temperature and SoC combination. Points in Fig. 3 represent the mean values of the four measured cells, while the horizontal and vertical error bars represent the standard deviation among the four cells for
ℜ
( Z )
and
− ℑ
( Z )
, respectively.

Frequencies above 5kHz are excluded from Fig. 3 to maintain clarity. At these frequencies,
− ℑ
( Z )
increases significantly, which would expand the y -axis scale and obscure the middle and low-frequency information.

Fig. 3 indicates that the impedance’s dependence on temperature becomes pronounced in the mid- and low-frequency ranges. Specifically, it can be observed that frequencies close to and higher than the “intercept frequency”
f
0
(as defined in [17]) remain almost temperature-independent.

In contrast, the typical near-semicircular arc obtained in the mid-frequency range (several hundred Hz down to approximately 1Hz) displays a clear temperature dependence. The impedance in this frequency range is dominated by the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) loss and the charge transfer loss of the anode, as well as the charge transfer loss of the cathode [52]. These losses arise from the charge transfer resistance at the interface between the anode (including the contribution of the SEI) or the cathode and the electrolyte during lithium-ion intercalation and deintercalation [52].

Similarly, the low-frequency range, recognized by its nearly linear tail in the Nyquist plot, also exhibits pronounced temperature dependence. In this frequency range, the impedance is mainly driven by the electrolyte and solid-state diffusion losses [52].

Dependence on SoC is less pronounced than temperature dependence and is not as readily discernible by visual inspection in a Nyquist diagram. Nonetheless, at the lowest SoC level a distinct increase in impedance appears in both the mid- and low-frequency ranges.

Cell-to-cell impedance variation remains nearly constant across all frequencies, temperatures, and SoC levels. Only a slight increase is evident in the mid- and low-frequency ranges at the lowest temperature, and no systematic variation with SoC is discernible.

Additionally, Fig. 4(a–d) illustrates the variance due to SoC (i, iv, vii), temperature (ii, v, viii), and cell-to-cell differences (iii, vi, ix) as functions of frequency for
ℜ
( Z )
(a) and
− ℑ
( Z )
(b),
ϕ
( Z )
(c), and
| Z |
(d) for the results of the isothermal measurements. Shaded regions around the mean lines represent the standard deviations.

The mean and standard deviation for SoC variation were calculated from the average values of the four measured cells across all SoC levels at the temperature indicated in the corresponding textbox. Likewise, the temperature variation mean and standard deviation were derived from the average of the four measured cells across all temperatures at the SoC indicated in the corresponding textbox. The cell-to-cell variance was obtained by averaging values for the four cells at the specific temperature and SoC shown in the textbox.

Based on the results from the isothermal measurements, it is evident that an optimal and robust feature selection based solely on visual inspection of the impedance spectra is not straightforward and would be prone to error. This limitation becomes even more apparent when the interaction between temperature and SoC, i.e., how the temperature dependence changes when SoC changes and vice versa, has to be considered.

Similar to the procedure used for the synthetic dataset obtained from the impedance model, ANOVA was applied to each feature. Because impedance spectra of the four cells were measured for every temperature-SoC combination during the isothermal measurements, the resulting dataset constituted a repeated-measures design (Section 3.2).

Fig. 4(A–D) displays the relative contributions to the total variance (i.e., ES) of temperature, SoC, and their interaction, together with the ES attributable to cell-to-cell variance and to unexplained variance. ES values whose corresponding
p
GG
value exceeds the threshold of 0.05 are neglected and set to 0 in Fig. 4(A–D).

Since
ω
ˆ
2
quantifies the proportion of the total variance explained by each variance source, it is inherently normalized, as expressed in Eq. (16). In this context, k indexes temperature, SoC, their interaction, cell-to-cell variance, and the unexplained variance. Hence a feature whose variance is largely explained by temperature (i.e.,
ω
ˆ
T
2
is large) must, by construction, exhibit correspondingly low relative contributions from the other variance sources, such as SoC or cell-to-cell variance. This additive constraint allows for a straightforward visualization of the relative contributions to the total variance (i.e., the ES) as shown in Fig. 4(A–D).

Consequently, the problem of searching for the optimal feature for temperature estimation reduces to identifying the feature with the largest
ω
ˆ
T
2
value. Ranking all features by their
ω
ˆ
T
2
therefore yields an immediate, interpretable feature classification for temperature estimation. The same procedure can be applied to
ω
ˆ
SoC
2
when the goal is SoC estimation. In Fig. 4, the top-ranked features are marked with a star and a downward-pointing triangle. The highest-ranked feature for temperature estimation is
ϕ
( Z )
at 356.5Hz with
ω
ˆ
T
2
= 0 . 98
, while the highest-ranked feature for SoC estimation is
ϕ
( Z )
at 0.80Hz with
ω
ˆ
SoC
2
= 0 . 88
.

It is worth noting that the feature ranking for temperature estimation does not necessarily point out the feature with the largest absolute variance caused by temperature; rather, it selects the feature with the highest ratio of temperature-driven variance relative to its total variance across all experimental conditions (as explained in Section 2.1). In other words, it highlights the feature whose variance is explained most strongly and most exclusively by temperature. This distinction illustrates the power of the systematic, ANOVA-based approach compared with a simple feature comparison based on the absolute variance due to temperature variation.

Notably, if feature selection were based solely on visual inspection of Fig. 4, as in several previous works discussed in Section 1, the likely conclusion would have been to select
ϕ
( Z )
at frequencies between 50Hz and 300Hz, since the variance due to temperature appears favorable compared to the variance due to SoC and cell-to-cell variability (Fig. 4(c)). This feature has indeed been used by many authors (see Table 1), and even if, in the best case, the outcome did not differ substantially from the conclusions obtained using our approach, identifying the feature whose variance is most strongly explained by temperature is not an easy visual task. Moreover, the inherent subjectivity and ambiguity of such visual inspection are evident, as is its susceptibility to error. These issues would become even more pronounced in the presence of additional IVs, such as SoH.

Once the feature ranking for temperature estimation was established using the isothermal measurements, an experiment under dynamic temperature and SoC conditions was conducted. The experiment was run on three cells of the same type and aging state as those previously used for the isothermal measurements (see Section 3.3) to test the applicability of the ANOVA-based feature selection approach presented in this work.

During this experiment, the highest-ranked and a mid-ranked feature in the temperature estimation ranking, together with the highest-ranked feature for SoC estimation, which is one of the lowest-ranked for temperature estimation, were continuously monitored.

Specifically,
ϕ
( Z )
at 356Hz (highest ES
T
),
ϕ
( Z )
at 10Hz (mid ES
T
, ranked 130th out of 258 in the temperature estimation feature ranking, with
ω
ˆ
T
2
= 0 . 08
), and
ϕ
( Z )
at 0.80Hz (highest ES
SoC
, ranked 219th out of 258 in the temperature estimation feature ranking, with
ω
ˆ
T
2
= 0 . 01
) were selected for the experiment. These selected features are highlighted in Fig. 4. Using the values of these selected features, the temperature throughout the experiment was estimated.

This experiment aims to validate the ranking obtained using ANOVA on the non-isothermal measurements. Notably, the mid ES
T
feature has been used for temperature estimation in previous works (see Table 1), further motivating its selection for the non-isothermal experiment.

For temperature estimation, a simple approach based on 2D look-up tables (LUTs) was applied. Each LUT was constructed using the isothermal measurement data, specifically the mean values from the four measured cells. Values at temperatures between the measured points were interpolated using a second-order polynomial fit via non-linear least squares. A resolution of 0.1°C was selected for the temperature values in the LUT.

The SoC was not considered in the temperature estimation process; hence, the LUT corresponding to 50% SoC was given to the estimation function regardless of the actual SoC during the experiment. Excluding SoC information from the estimation function enables an assessment of the impact of SoC on the selected features for temperature estimation and, consequently, a validation of the ANOVA-based feature ranking, given the normalized nature of the ES values.

Furthermore, in practical applications, a temperature estimation method that does not rely on SoC information is preferable, as it remains robust against SoC estimation errors and eliminates the need for impedance measurements at multiple SoC levels for LUT construction.

The estimated temperature was obtained by identifying the closest feature value in the LUT and returning the associated temperature. The rationale for using this simple approach was to reveal and compare the intrinsic potential of the features ranked by ANOVA for temperature estimation, while deliberately avoiding more complex strategies that might obscure differences between the features.
Fig. 5(a) compares the estimated temperature using the three selected features against the measured temperature and the SoC during the experiment for one of the three cells.

As shown in Fig. 5(a), the estimated temperature based on the highest ES
T
feature closely follows the measured temperature across the entire temperature range, showing minimal dependence on SoC variations. The performance of this feature for temperature estimation is further summarized in Fig. 5(b), where the estimation error and the RMSE for the three features are illustrated. The highest ES
T
feature achieves an RMSE of 1.25°C.

In contrast, the mid ES
T
feature performs well at low and mid temperatures but deviates significantly at the highest temperature, showing an unreliable estimation and a total RMSE of 6°C. Finally, the highest ES
SoC
feature yields poor performance across the entire temperature range, with an RMSE of 14.3°C.
These results are consistent across all three investigated cells in the non-isothermal experiment. The results for the remaining two cells are presented in Figs. A.7 and A.8 (see Appendix).

The observed performance of the three features for temperature estimation can be interpreted by examining their respective LUTs, shown in Fig. 5 (c–e). The LUT corresponding to the highest ES
T
feature (c) exhibits a clear, quasi-linear dependence on temperature across the full range and minimal SoC dependence across the experiment SoC range. Consequently, the implemented LUT-based approach is able to accurately estimate the temperature using this feature. The low SoC dependence explains the estimation robustness despite the SoC variations during the experiment.

Conversely, the LUT corresponding to the mid ES
T
feature shows a well-defined temperature dependence only below 30°C, transitioning to a non-bijective shape at higher temperatures. This non-bijective relationship between
ϕ
( Z )
and temperature explains the estimation instability at the highest temperature, with values jumping between two distinct temperature levels. Lastly, the LUT for the highest ES
SoC
feature remains nearly constant over the temperature range, indicating that temperature estimation based on this feature is infeasible.

In light of these results, the question arises whether the SoC can be estimated with similar accuracy. A comparison of the highest ES values for temperature and SoC estimation, 0.98 and 0.88, respectively, suggests that SoC estimation based on a single feature is inherently less reliable than temperature estimation.

Fig. 6 (a–c) presents the 2D LUTs for SoC estimation corresponding to the three selected features. Despite similar variance due to SoC variation across all three features, the strong dependence of the highest ES
T
feature on temperature would require highly accurate temperature knowledge for SoC estimation. In other words, even a slight discrepancy between the monitored and actual cell temperature would cause significant SoC estimation errors.

In contrast, the highest ES
SoC
feature displays weak temperature dependence (Fig. 6(c)), making it more robust for SoC estimation across the entire experiment temperature range. This finding underscores the limitations of feature selection based solely on absolute variance metrics, particularly in applications involving multi-dimensional dependencies.

However, the strongly non-linear shape of the LUT corresponding to the highest ES
SoC
feature complicates direct SoC estimation using the same LUT-based approach as for temperature. Thus, a more advanced estimation method would be required, which lies beyond the scope of this work.

This work presents an approach for feature selection in LIB state estimation using impedance data. Specifically, the approach is demonstrated for temperature estimation, since the extensive available literature on this topic reveals a clear gap regarding systematic feature selection methods. The approach is based on ANOVA and quantifies the ES of the different variance sources in the impedance data, thereby enabling a systematic, reproducible, and straightforward feature selection process.

For the methodology development and initial validation, a synthetic impedance dataset generated by a DFN-based impedance model was used. The model allowed for a targeted variance definition across the simulated frequency range, enabling a clear evaluation of the ANOVA results. Subsequently, the approach was applied to experimental impedance data obtained from measurements under isothermal conditions at different temperatures and SoC levels. The ANOVA results were used to rank the impedance features according to their ES for temperature. The highest-ranked, a mid-ranked, and a low-ranked feature were selected for temperature estimation in a non-isothermal experiment under dynamic temperature and SoC conditions. The results demonstrated the potential of the ANOVA-based feature selection approach for temperature estimation, with the highest-ranked feature achieving an RMSE of 1.25°C without SoC information.

The proposed methodology applies ANOVA to a set of impedance features in the impedance dataset. Using the results, the ES of each IV for every feature is quantified. Since the ES corresponding to temperature at a certain feature is defined as a ratio of the amount of variance explained by the temperature to the total variance, a feature with a high ES for temperature necessarily exhibits a low ES for the other variance sources, such as SoC or cell-to-cell variation. This property allows for a straightforward ranking of features for temperature estimation. Similarly, features can be ranked according to different IVs, enabling the transferability of the approach to other applications, such as SoC estimation.

The use of ANOVA for feature selection eliminates the need for subjective interpretation of impedance spectra through visual inspection, which remains the predominant approach in current literature and is prone to inaccuracies. Additionally, ANOVA enables the consideration of all possible IVs interactions for feature selection, with nearly constant selection complexity and effort regardless of the number of IVs and features. This is a significant advantage compared to visual inspection, where complexity increases multiplicatively with the number of IVs, becoming impractical for more than two IVs and four impedance components. Moreover, ANOVA intrinsically considers the variance due to cell-to-cell differences and measurement noise in the feature selection process, which, to the best of our knowledge, has not been addressed in previous works. All of these aspects represent a significant advancement in feature selection methodology for impedance-based battery state estimation, with applicability extending beyond temperature estimation to other critical battery states such as SoC and SoH.

However, ANOVA results may be affected by the DoE when the relationship between an IV and impedance is non-linear. Since ANOVA compares group means across discrete levels of the IVs, such relationships may go undetected if the chosen levels do not adequately capture the underlying behavior. To ensure reliable results, the number and spacing of levels should be selected according to the expected non-linearity in the IV-impedance relationship. Prior knowledge and a carefully designed DoE are therefore essential for meaningful application of ANOVA.

In this work, four cells were measured for each temperature and SoC combination, which we consider to be sufficient for representing cell-to-cell variation. However, future work should investigate the impact of the number of measured cells on the feature selection results.

Furthermore, in this study ANOVA-based feature selection is demonstrated for a full factorial experiment design. Future work is necessary to investigate the impact of reduced experimental designs on the feature selection results.

Notably, in addition to the impedance features compared in this work, many further features, such as characteristic points of the Nyquist plot or the ECM parameters extracted from the impedance data, could be considered in future work. This could result in the identification of even more performant features for temperature and SoC estimation.

The results presented in this work, and in particular the feature ranking obtained for impedance-based temperature estimation, are derived from a dataset corresponding to a specific cell chemistry and format. Consequently, the universality of the reported feature ranking cannot be assumed a priori. Further investigations based on datasets obtained from cells with different chemistries and formats are therefore required to assess the generality of the presented results.

Nevertheless, even if cell chemistry and format affect the feature selection outcome, the systematic and robust ANOVA-based feature selection framework proposed in this work remains highly valuable, enabling consistent and repeatable analyses across different cell types relevant to a given application. Moreover, applying the proposed framework to multiple cell types could enable a comparative analysis, potentially revealing which chemistries or formats are more suitable for impedance-based temperature estimation or for the estimation of additional battery states, such as SoC and SoH.

Finally, the results presented in this work are constrained to a dataset obtained from fresh cells without variations in SoH. Since the impact of SoH on impedance characteristics has been widely reported in the literature, the feature ranking obtained for temperature estimation may quantitatively change, or additional features may emerge as optimal, when SoH variation is included in the analysis. Investigating the influence of aging on the feature selection results is therefore necessary and constitutes a priority for future work.

In this context, the nearly constant complexity of the ANOVA-based feature selection approach proposed in this work, regardless of the number of considered IVs, represents a key advantage when incorporating SoH as an additional source of variability.
